main_summary.summary: Simeon Wright, cousin of Emmett Till and eyewitness to the August 1955 kidnapping in Money, Mississippi, recorded by Joe Mosnier in Argo, Illinois on May 23, 2011 for the Smithsonian and Library of Congress Civil Rights Oral History project. Wright, twelve years old at the time, describes his parents Moses and Elizabeth Wright, the arrival of Emmett Till and Wheeler Parker Jr. on the family's Mississippi farm, the Wednesday August 24, 1955 visit to Bryant's Store, Till's whistle at Carolyn Bryant, and the early-morning kidnapping by Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam. He recounts identifying Emmett's ring as a material witness, sitting in the witness room during the Sumner trial, and his father's testimony naming Milam in court. Wright describes the family's relocation to Argo, Illinois after the not-guilty verdict, his religious conversion at age twenty-four in 1967, his pipefitter career at Reynolds Metals, the 2004-2005 federal reopening of the case with Keith Beauchamp and Alvin Sykes, the exhumation and DNA identification at Burr Oak Cemetery, the Till Bill legislation, and his decision to write Simeon's Story to correct what he calls the inaccuracies in published accounts.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Wright is one of the last surviving direct eyewitnesses to the events at Bryant's Store and the kidnapping from the Wright family home in Money, Mississippi. He shared a bed with Emmett Till the night of the abduction and was present for both the store encounter and Milam and Bryant entering the bedroom. His testimony directly contradicts several widely circulated accounts of the case, including claims that the cousins dared Till to enter the store, that Till had photographs of white girlfriends, that Till touched Carolyn Bryant inside the store, that Maurice Wright accepted fifty cents store credit to help locate the house, and James Hicks's account that Moses Wright escaped Mississippi in a coffin.

The interview also documents the 2004-2005 federal reopening of the case: Wright's meeting with U.S. Attorney Greenleaf, the role of filmmaker Keith Beauchamp and Alvin Sykes, the exhumation at Burr Oak Cemetery in Chicago where Wright served as the DNA donor, prosecutor Joyce Childs's grand-jury proceeding against Carolyn Bryant, and the passage of the Till Bill funding federal investigation of civil-rights-era cold cases. Wright's first-person account of his father's decision to testify against Milam in open court, supported by Medgar Evers and the NAACP, establishes Moses Wright's testimony as the documented act it was rather than the embellished version in some published sources.

chapters.title: Born and Bred in Mississippi
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program with videographer John Bishop for the Smithsonian and Library of Congress in Argo, Illinois, on May 23, 2011, Simeon Wright opens with his parents: a hard but fair cotton farmer who vowed he was born and bred in Mississippi and would die there, and his easy, singing mother, raised in Hazlehurst and married in Sumner in 1925.

chapters.title: To Correct the Inaccuracies
chapters.summary: Wright explains that after watching documentaries that presented untruths about the Emmett Till case, his wife urged him to write Simeon's Story to tell what happened at the store and in his bedroom and to rebut myths, including the false claims that he and his brother Maurice dared Emmett to speak to Carolyn Bryant and that his father escaped Mississippi in a coffin.

chapters.title: Someone From the North
chapters.summary: Nearly thirteen in the late August of 1955, Wright describes the excitement when he learned cousins Emmett Till and Wheeler Parker Jr. were coming from Chicago, Emmett a great storyteller who told of Lincoln Park and North Avenue Beach, places Wright still visits with his Sunday school picnic.

chapters.title: How You Swim in Mississippi
chapters.summary: Wright recounts welcoming Emmett and Wheeler, sharing a bed with Emmett, and teaching them country ways: running snakes out of the water before swimming and borrowing a neighbor's watermelons, and he refutes the false account that the boys stole his father's car and drove into Money, noting his father had stopped preaching in 1949.

chapters.title: Emmett Whistled at Her
chapters.summary: Picking up after a recording pause, Wright fixes the date as Wednesday, August 24, 1955, and describes the brief trip to Bryant's Store with his brother Maurice, Wheeler, Emmett, and Roosevelt Crawford, where Maurice sent him in to watch Emmett, who paid for his items, and as Carolyn Bryant walked to her car Emmett whistled at her, sending the terrified boys racing for the car.

chapters.title: She Made That Up
chapters.summary: Wright lists the six boys who went, names another possible neighbor he is unsure of, and describes the wooden-floored store with its counter, rejecting Carolyn Bryant's trial claim that Emmett put his arms around her and asked for a date, testimony she gave when not under oath because, as he believes, her lawyers knew it could be perjury.

chapters.title: He Pulled His Gun and Made Him Close
chapters.summary: Wright explains that stores in Money closed early on Wednesdays, and the family ordinarily would not shop at Bryant's, but Bryant kept his open in defiance of the rule, once even pulling a gun to make another owner close, and he reaffirms that Emmett completed a routine purchase and whistled only when Carolyn came out toward her car.

chapters.title: Don't Tell Daddy
chapters.summary: Wright tells of fleeing down the seven-mile Dark Fear Road, his brother Maurice stopping when headlights appeared that turned out to be neighbors, and Emmett begging the boys not to tell his father, having no notion he could be killed for the whistle; Wright also recalls Carolyn gave a long look as they ran.

chapters.title: None of That Happened
chapters.summary: Wright disputes the circulated stories that the boys lollygagged outside, that Emmett carried a photo of a white girlfriend, and that the cousins dared him to enter, noting a nephew said so on film for Eyes on the Prize though he was not there, and recalls the neighbor girl Rutha Mae Crawford warning the next day that they would hear more about it.

chapters.title: This Is the Wrong Boy
chapters.topic: Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam Come
chapters.summary: After a Saturday trip to Greenwood, Wright describes how about two a.m. Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam came to the four-bedroom house, woke Wheeler and said they wanted the fat boy from Chicago, then marched to Wright's room where Milam, with gun and flashlight, made Emmett dress; his mother pleaded and offered money, Milam threatened his sixty-four-year-old father, and a woman's voice, which Wright believes was Carolyn Bryant's, confirmed Emmett as the right one.

chapters.title: I Can't Stay Here Another Night
chapters.topic: The Aftermath and the Call to Chicago
chapters.summary: Wright recalls that he never slept again that night, his mother ran to a straw boss named Chamblee who would not get involved, and with no phone and no police his father drove her to her brother's home in Sumner, from which she later left for Chicago and never returned to the house; he describes using the bossman's telephone and how a boy who would later falsely claim to have been at the store had in fact arrived that night, slept through the kidnapping, and phoned Wright's oldest sister Willie Mae, who reached Emmett's mother Mamie.

chapters.title: There Will Be No Burial Here Today
chapters.topic: The Body Found and Sheriff Smith
chapters.summary: Wright recounts staying with neighbors who owned their own land, returning to picking cotton while hoping Emmett was alive, and the discovery of the body in the Tallahatchie River about twenty miles north; when officials tried to bury it the same day, Leflore County sheriff George Smith halted the burial, a man Wright understands as fairer and unwilling to carry that stain.

chapters.title: Strider Testified for the Defense
chapters.summary: Wright describes Sheriff Strider testifying for the defense and reversing himself on whether the river body was Black or white, the murder indictment sought in Tallahatchie County where they felt they had a better chance, and his own role identifying Emmett's ring (Bobo's ring) as a subpoenaed material witness, though in the end Mamie's identification meant he was not called.

chapters.title: They Lost a Way of Life
chapters.topic: The Witness Room and Where Hatred Came From
chapters.summary: Wright recalls the circus atmosphere of the witness room, where the bailiff was unusually kind and he felt sure, from cowboy movies, that a conviction was a done deal, then reflects that the jury and judge's racism ran deeper than skin: a hatred he traces to the Civil War and a lost aristocratic way of life passed down through generations, unlike the white boys he played and hunted snakes with who were not born racist, and his refusal to start calling a playmate named Tommy Peterson Mister.

chapters.title: We Can't Stay Here Any Longer
chapters.topic: The Verdict and His Father's Courage
chapters.summary: Picking up after a recording pause, Wright describes the not-guilty verdict that crushed his father and the segregationists' rejoicing, his father's decision to leave, and the courage of his father, warned by neighbors he would be killed and encouraged by Medgar Evers of the NAACP, to testify anyway, doing what no other Black man in Mississippi had done and lived.

chapters.title: No Job for a Sixty-Three-Year-Old
chapters.summary: Wright contrasts Mississippi life, where his father had winters to hunt and fish and once cleared six thousand dollars in 1948 under a fair German born-again Christian bossman, with the move to Argo Summit where factories would not hire a man past sixty and his father could only find restaurant cleanup work.

chapters.title: She Never Sang Again
chapters.summary: Wright recalls living rent-free in his brother James's four-flat, then describes how the murder took years off his mother, a churchwoman who sang every day in Mississippi but fell silent in Chicago, comforted only by a sense that the Lord had spared her family, and he defends her education against a writer who called her uneducated, recalling the stories she told of the Lindbergh kidnapping and a tracker called Hawkjaw Mullin.

chapters.title: If They Slap Us, We Slap Back
chapters.summary: Wright recalls Martin Luther King bringing his nonviolent message to Chicago and Marquette Park, and how he and his friends, knowing what Mississippi segregationists did, refused to march under a rule that they could not retaliate if slapped or spit on, until at age twenty-four, sitting half high in a tavern, he heard a voice warn that if he died in his sins he would go to hell, which began to change his life.

chapters.title: Harder Than Picking Cotton
chapters.summary: Wright traces his working life from shining shoes on the street and in a parlor in Wheeler Parker's barbershop, to setting bowling pins at a hundred and five pounds, to Dearborn Glass, and finally to Reynolds Metals, where he entered a pipefitting apprenticeship under Union Local 597 and worked from 1968 until retiring in 1994, near the August anniversary of Emmett's abduction.

chapters.title: Something I Want
chapters.summary: Wright explains that most of his mother's side stayed quiet about the case while his father's side sometimes raised it, and describes how he came to commit his life to Christ by watching the joy and peace in his cousin Wheeler Parker after Wheeler's own conversion; Parker, whose wedding he stood up in as best man, is now his pastor and Wright is a deacon who teaches second and third graders.

chapters.title: You're Calling Me a Liar
chapters.topic: The Inaccuracies That Brought Him Back
chapters.summary: Wright tells how the case drew him back when reporters merged what he told them with false history, and he names the inaccuracies that anger him: that his father escaped in a coffin, that the cousins dared Emmett, that Emmett did not whistle, and James Hicks's claim that his father said Thar he, when the transcript shows he said There he is.

chapters.title: He Would Lay Across Your Threshold
chapters.summary: Wright recounts how filmmaker Keith Beauchamp, whom his wife urged him to talk to, drew out his account and, with Alvin Sykes, pieced together federal violations that justified reopening the case, leading to a meeting with the Northern District U.S. Attorney Mr. Greenleaf, who was visibly shaken and kept his promise to seek a reinvestigation jointly with Mississippi.

chapters.title: We Agreed on That Already
chapters.summary: Wright explains that Mississippi required proof the body at Burr Oak Cemetery was Emmett's, so the body was exhumed despite a protesting cousin he says was put up to it by so-called civil rights workers seeking TV time, and DNA testing, for which he was the secret donor, confirmed the identity and showed Emmett had not been castrated nor his teeth knocked out as some had said.

chapters.title: Not Enough Information
chapters.summary: Wright recounts that after tracing the gun and shells back to Bryant and Milam, prosecutor Joyce Childs said there was not enough information to indict Carolyn Bryant despite a witness placing her in the truck that night, that the FBI rather than Childs questioned Bryant and that Bryant sought immunity to talk but was refused, leaving Wright greatly disappointed.

chapters.part: The Till Bill and the Tellers
chapters.title: In Honor of Emmett Till
chapters.summary: Wright describes how Alvin Sykes of Kansas City pursued legislation through a senator to fund the pursuit of unsettled cold cases, how he and his wife drove to Washington and watched from the gallery as the bill stalled over one holdout senator before passing months later with about a million dollars, and his lingering wish that Mississippi or Leflore County would call the verdict unjust.
related_events: Emmett Till Unsolved Civil Rights Crime Act

chapters.part: The Till Bill and the Tellers
chapters.title: Thar He
chapters.topic: James Hicks and the Coffin Story
chapters.summary: Wright examines why even scholarly tellings went askew, focusing on James Hicks, the Black press reporter who claimed his father pointed at J. W. Milam and said Thar he when the transcript reads There he is, and who also spread the coffin-escape story with Medgar Evers, which Wright rebuts by asking how his father's three sons got out if there was only one coffin.

chapters.part: The Till Bill and the Tellers
chapters.title: He Never Interviewed Us
chapters.summary: Wright rejects William Bradford Huie's Look magazine writing, the claim that he looked at Wright's mother and saw she was uneducated and a fabricated interview in Argo in which the family supposedly showed Huie where a white girlfriend lived, and he likewise dismisses a Hampton, Virginia play and the Emerge Magazine story that Maurice took fifty cents of store credit to lead the killers to the house, insisting the family had no credit at Bryant's.

chapters.title: If You Die in Your Sins
chapters.summary: Returning to the conversion he described earlier, Wright retells how at twenty-four, after King came to town in 1966 and he refused to march, he heard a voice in a tavern about two a.m. warning that if he died in his sins he would go to hell, and within two weeks in February 1967 quit drinking, smoking, and his girlfriend, committed his life to Christ, and came to see nonviolence as the only way to change a country.

chapters.title: Vengeance to God, Justice to the Government
chapters.summary: Wright explains what it meant to forgive Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam: not surrendering the claim to justice but leaving vengeance to Almighty God, a difficult process rooted in the warning that if he did not forgive he would not be forgiven, and he describes how smells like honeysuckle and the sound of a passing car still bring back the night and the grief, leaving scar tissue on a healed heart.

chapters.title: Laws Can't Change a Man's Heart
chapters.summary: Wright invokes Jeremiah at the potter's house as an image of his own remade life and assesses the case's legacy: federal law that trumps state law brought changes, but hearts have changed little and racism, though no longer in your face as under Jim Crow, persists in money-gated neighborhoods, even as new generations horrified by Emmett's story, like the prosecutor who convicted the Birmingham church bombers, vow to bring change.

chapters.title: I'm From This Dirt
chapters.summary: Wright reflects that Mississippi is both a place of horror and the home of his childhood, that he visits but would not live there, and he closes with his message to young people to go to school, respect their elders, love one another, and believe the system can change, while decrying a Chicago police beating of a bartender and insisting that whoever commits the crime should do the time, Black or white.

loc_video.caption: Simeon Wright oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Chicago, Illinois, 2011 May 23
